How long have you been in treatment now at Bronson here? At Bronson, I've only been six months since I switched over from him before to Bronson. And can you tell me maybe something, you know, people don't expect to go through cancer treatment, whether it might be the medication or maybe how you feel, whether it might be your energy wise, you know, can you just tell me it was something that people don't usually expect when they're going through cancer treatment? For me, I didn't expect the neuropathy. I got neuropathy in my hands and my feet. And that was part of the chemo. They discontinued that, but the problem is here now. But that particular treatment, it was nausea and no appetite. It was this terrible experience at first, but it's things that mellowed out. And I don't have any outward symptoms other than neuropathy. When I first met you, you were just already just a very positive person, even at a very small amount of time. Can you tell me how you've been able to remain positive, not being bitter, despite going through your terminal diagnosis? What kind of keeps you on the positive side of things? Well, my age, for one, I'm older now, so I look at life differently now. I appreciate every day that I'm blessed to wake up and see another day. So I don't wake up, I'm open and moaning because of my condition. I just, no matter how uncomfortable I'm feeling that day, I stay positive and move forward. Because I'm able to, I mean, with my neuropathy, my hands and my feet are killing me, but I'm still able to walk. So I push forward, you know, my hands, I can't do buttons and zippers and I have trouble opening a candy bar because I can't feel what I still move on. And I'm thankful and blessed to have the support of family, and that gives me strength too. The family support. Kind of taking a step back, glad you mentioned family support. When you were first diagnosed, how did your family, your friends, your general community come and be supportive to you? Well, since my mother and everyone else died from cancer, the rally support is there. Like when my mother was going through cancer, people will fly in from my family, take shifts from week to week to take care of her. What do you like to personally do to stay both like physically active but also mentally and like socially active? I participate in yoga at the hospital twice a week and I do a little strong.
